comments.author: @WhereonEarth4K
comments.text:
👇 Everything we curated for this MV Hondius episode. Books, historical context, travel resources.

*📚 BROWSE THE SOUTH ATLANTIC COLLECTION*
  ◦ Every book and resource we curated for this episode → https://amzn.to/4v0So2N

*🛍 SEE OUR FULL W.O.E. STOREFRONT*
  ◦ Every destination Collection in one place → https://www.amazon.com/shop/whereonearth

*🎟 ANTARCTIC EXPEDITION CRUISES FROM USHUAIA*
  ◦ Polar expedition tours departing the end of the world → https://shorturl.at/WpbkU
  ◦ Browse all Patagonia experiences → https://shorturl.at/a8ehB

*📖 PLAN YOUR PATAGONIA TRIP*
  ◦ Lonely Planet Argentina, current 13th edition → https://amzn.to/4fib498

*📚 GO DEEPER INTO THE STORY*
  ◦ Spillover by David Quammen, the canonical book on zoonotic disease → https://amzn.to/4wXtxho
  ◦ The Coming Plague by Laurie Garrett, Pulitzer Prize-winning history of emerging diseases → https://amzn.to/4vkjQbs
  ◦ In Patagonia by Bruce Chatwin, the masterpiece of travel writing on the land where Andes virus lives → https://amzn.to/43KvujP

*🖼 THE VISUAL JOURNEY*
  ◦ The Endurance by Caroline Alexander, with Frank Hurley's photographs of Shackleton's expedition → https://amzn.to/4dGLppq
  ◦ Sebastião Salgado: Genesis, a love letter to remote places and indigenous peoples → https://amzn.to/4a6Zfz1

comments.author: @aidasegal588
comments.text: I’ve been in Ushuaia in 2006.. Amazing part of the world.. within 15 min.weather changes tree times: from snow flakes to rain and sun you take almost all  layers of clothes

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Fifteen minutes and three seasons — that's Ushuaia in a sentence. There's nowhere quite like standing at the edge of the world. Did you make it any further south from there?

comments.author: @alidabotes6264
comments.text: It sounds similar to the Ebola virus.

comments.author: @nanetharty357
comments.text: The dumbest thing they could’ve done was taken anyone off of that ship. It’s contained for the most part separate the sick from the healthy, but nobody gets off the ship.  That’s called triage.  Because if you let people off of that ship, you are now going to infect the rest of the world.  And whoever gave the order to take people off that ship should be thrown in jail.  Because that is not how you protect the masses.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That tension is exactly what the film circles — the impossible calculus between containment and compassion when the clock is running. Which decision in the whole story surprised you most?

replies.author: @alidabotes6264
replies.text: Imagine how we as South Africans feel. They could have flown that patient to the Netherlands.

comments.author: @EpicEarthYTB
comments.text: Wow, the way this video connects Napoleon's exile to a modern-day medical evacuation is absolutely fascinating! I never knew Ascension Island’s runway existed for such a specific historical reason. Chilling but incredibly well-told story.

comments.author: @oldodger
comments.text:
So much to be learned from your presentation. Knowledge to be used to put some perceived threats in real
perspectives so not to panic with here say and  misinformation.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That's exactly what this story deserves — not panic, but context. Thank you for watching. 🌿

comments.author: @user-Rocket-Fest
comments.text: so cruise ship cooking has never harmed or killed anyone ??? Its always some random ''Virus'' from some random Port , yeh sure.

comments.author: @elizabethcampbell9888
comments.text: In June 1993 my mother and I were to go on a volunteer trip to a Navajo school in New Mexico in support of maintaining their heritage. We were so saddened to learn a virus, resulting in so many deaths, prevented us from going. We later found out it was Hanta.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thank you for sharing that... it's a quiet reminder that the 1993 outbreak wasn't a distant event but something that touched real plans and real families. I hope you and your mother found another way to support the school in the years after. Comments like yours are part of why this story felt worth telling.

comments.author: @jeremy1350
comments.text:
The only reason this became an issue was because a "White Western Tourist" brought it out of a land fill, watching birds over a trash dump, and onto a ship. 
A disease that is endemic in areas where the White Western Man pays no attention gets NO attention, until it crosses into a group of tourists. 
And then hysteria breaks out, and everybody thinks "Here we go again!!" Nuff Said ...

replies.author: @jeremy1350
replies.text: @WhereonEarth4K Exactly. And those of us who follow virology and epidemiology and lived through AIDS can see the writing on the walls everybody else misses, or refuses to acknowledge. Sadly, this goes both ways. History tells us that the world would have been very different then "The White Man Came!!" Now Indigenous communities who are ignored by the world around it, says now "And then the White Man Came!!"  Poetic, isn't it ??

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: You've put your finger on the exact thing the film is about. The same virus had been killing rural and Indigenous people across Patagonia for decades with almost no attention... Epuyén lost eleven people in 2018 and the world barely noticed. It took a cruise ship full of international tourists for the surveillance machinery to move in 26 days. The unequal attention isn't a side note to this story. It's the story.

comments.author: @MadMiff
comments.text: WTF is this shit, birdwatcher, not birder. You twater AI click baiter
